Magnetic Resonance Imaging for Diagnosis of Soft Tissue and Osseous Injuries in the Horse
=========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Natasha M.Werpy DVM

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1053/j.ctep.2005.02.017](https://doi.org/10.1053/j.ctep.2005.02.017 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1534751605000272&orderBeanReset=true)

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is becoming readily available to equine veterinarians. As with any new [imaging modality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/imaging-modality "Learn more about imaging modality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") it is necessary to understand the uses, limitations, and diagnostic capabilities. One of the most valuable uses of MRI in adult horses is the ability to image the soft tissues of the extremities, providing diagnostic information not possible with any other currently available imaging modality. MRI provides both anatomic and physiologic tissue characteristics allowing diagnosis of soft tissue and osseous injuries. This chapter provides an introduction to basic MRI principles: safety and technical considerations, physics, sequences, tissue appearance on images, and image interpretation. Specific examples of normal anatomic structures and structures with pathologic change are provided. Determining the normal appearance of structures with age and exercise is necessary for image interpretation and identification of pathologic change. Collaboration will expedite this process and benefit clinicians and patients. Soft tissue and osseous injuries characterized by using MRI will allow specific diagnosis, appropriate and targeted treatment, rehabilitation, and monitoring.

Safety and Technical Considerations
-----------------------------------

This is a brief summary of safety and technical considerations. This is an important subject that cannot be adequately addressed in the scope of this article and is extensively covered in the literature. Engineering of most MRI systems dictates that the magnetic field is present even when the system is not in use. The magnetic field extends beyond the magnet housing. The intensity and size of the field is dependent on the magnet field strength. The 5-gauss line is commonly marked because

Basic MRI Physics
-----------------

The main components of an MRI system include the magnet, the gradient coils, the RF coils, and the computer software system. The magnet creates the main magnetic field. The RF coils can receive and/or transmit RF pulses resulting in the signal the computer uses to create images. The protons within the tissues align along the magnetic field when the patient is placed in the magnet. The radiofrequency pulse produced by the RF coil is used to change the orientation of the protons to varying

Terminology and Sequences
-------------------------

The imaging sequence used determines the appearance, or shade of gray, of different tissue types. “Signal intensity” is the term used to describe how bright or dark a tissue type appears on MR images. Tissues that are white are described as hyperintense or high signal intensity. Conversely, hypointense or low signal intensity tissues appear black. Isointense can be used in two ways. It can be used to describe a gray structure or two structures that have the same intensity. On a T2-weighted

Clinical Applications and Lesion Identification
-----------------------------------------------

In adult horses, MRI is a useful procedure for examination of the soft tissues of the extremities.3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 Structures that are difficult or impossible to image conventionally because of location or superimposition can be examined in multiple planes using MRI (Fig 5).10, 11 Diagnosis of injury to structures of the foot is possible using MRI (Fig 6). Lameness localized to the foot is a common problem and many lame horses have normal radiographic and scintigraphic studies. The diagnosis 

Conclusion
----------

Magnetic resonance imaging allows detection of soft tissue lesions in structures that were previously not readily examined. The normal appearance of these structures, accounting for age and exercise, must be determined for optimal image evaluation and diagnosis. Establishing the imaging sequences that are sensitive and specific for detecting lesions will advance the usefulness of MRI. Identification of pathologic change and knowledge of the clinical relevance of these findings is necessary for

--- SOURCE 2: Advantages and areas of application of MRI in equine ... ---
URL: https://www.pferdeklinik.ch/en/facilities/mri/

SUMMARY:
MRI can detect changes in tissue long before clinical symptoms appear. This enables early intervention and can significantly improve the prognosis. Treatment

FULL CONTENT:
![MRI](/fileadmin/_processed_/6/d/csm_IMG_2570_f6b06c9d23.jpg)

# MRI

## Advantages and areas of application of MRI in equine medicine

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has established itself as a very useful diagnostic tool in equine medicine, the advantages and areas of application of which are explained below:

Detailed Images  
MRI provides extremely detailed images of internal structures. In to X-rays, which mainly show bone, MRI can visualize both soft tissue and bone tissue. This is particularly useful for diagnosing soft tissue changes and inflammation in the limbs that cause lameness, for example.

Detection of soft tissue damage  
MRI is particularly valuable for the detection and assessment of soft tissue damage, such as tendon or ligament injuries. Such injuries can be difficult to detect with other imaging techniques. ist besonders wertvoll für die Erkennung und Bewertung von Weichteilschäden.

Confirm or rule out previous diagnoses  
MRI can be used to confirm or refute the results of other imaging techniques, leading to a more accurate diagnosis and targeted therapy.

Non-invasive Method  
As this is a non-invasive method, the risk to the horse is minimal and the examination is not painful. No incisions or surgical procedures are required, which reduces the risk of infection.

Early detection of diseases  
MRI can detect changes in tissue long before clinical symptoms appear. This enables early intervention and can significantly improve the prognosis.

Treatment planning and monitoring  
After diagnosis, MRI can be used to monitor the progress of treatment. Changes in the size and nature of injuries can be closely monitored, allowing the treatment plan to be adjusted.  
  
The use of MRI in equine medicine requires specialised equipment and expertise, as the size and behaviour of the animals present particular challenges. Despite these challenges, MRI provides invaluable diagnostic support that contributes to the health and well-being of horses.

## Procedure

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an advanced imaging technique that is frequently used to diagnose diseases in horses, particularly in the area of the legs. MRI uses strong magnetic fields and radio waves to produce detailed images of structures inside the body and, unlike computed tomography (CT), does not involve radiation. Here is a detailed description of the procedure for an MRI scan on a horse:

![Preparation](/fileadmin/_processed_/2/7/csm_DSC_4649_b802b42739.jpg?default)

### Preparation

Medical history and preliminary examination: Before the MRI examination, the vet will carry out a thorough clinical examination of the horse and check the medical history. This is important to ensure that the horse is suitable for the MRI.

![Sedation and anaesthesia](/fileadmin/_processed_/9/d/csm_DSC_6431_35b7dbdc75.jpg?default)

### Sedation and anaesthesia

The MRI examination requires the horse to be absolutely still throughout the procedure. Therefore, the horse is usually sedated or a general anaesthetic is used.  
Monitoring the horse during anaesthesia is crucial to ensure that it remains stable and no complications occur.

![Positioning in the MRI machine](/fileadmin/_processed_/6/d/csm_IMG_2570_f8d470261b.jpg?default)

### Positioning in the MRI machine

The horse is carefully positioned on the MRI scanner and the region to be examined is positioned in the coil. In our MRI, the limbs up to and including the knee joint can be examined.  
It is important that the horse is positioned correctly in order to achieve excellent image quality.

![Performing the MRI](/fileadmin/_processed_/1/c/csm_IMG_2589_e097b98cf9.jpg?default)

### Performing the MRI

Once the horse is positioned, the actual MRI examination begins. A strong magnetic field is generated around the region to be examined, which aligns the water molecules in the body in parallel. A radio wave pulse is then applied, which changes the effect of the hydrogen nuclei. After this pulse, they return to their original parallel position. The extent of this deflection is recorded and converted into sectional images by a computer.  
Depending on the size of the area to be examined and the desired level of detail of the images, the MRI can take between 30 minutes and several hours.

![After the examination](/fileadmin/_processed_/2/a/csm_DSC_4568_4989acd44c.jpg?default)

### After the examination

After the images have been taken, the horse is slowly awakened from the anaesthetic if a general anaesthetic has been administered.  
The horse will be monitored until it is fully awake from sedation or anaesthesia to ensure that there are no adverse reactions or complications.  
The MRI images are analysed by our specialists in order to make a diagnosis or plan further treatment steps.

--- SOURCE 3: Use of Magnetic Resonance Imaging in the Horse ---
URL: https://vetmed-extension-a2.sites.medinfo.ufl.edu/files/2011/10/HHC-proceedings-Brokken.pdf

SUMMARY:
MRI allows diagnosis of both bone and soft tissue (ligament/tendon) problems that were not previously possible in horses with the use of other imaging

FULL CONTENT:
Use of Magnetic Resonance Imaging in the Horse Matthew T. Brokken, DVM, DACVS Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has been a cornerstone for musculoskeletal imaging in humans for over 20 years. Until recently, there were only a small number of horses being imaged with MRI. As information has spread about the benefits of MRI in horses, so have the number of facilities with magnetic resonance units available for our equine patients. We are still in the infancy of equine MRI but it has already proven to be an extremely valuable tool for diagnosis and treatment of horses with performance limiting lameness. MRI is a proton based imaging modality. Briefly, the horse is placed in the magnet under general anesthesia, radiowaves are pulsed into the patient, the patient emits a signal, and the signal is used to construct an image. The examination takes about an hour and a half and generates 600-800 images. Due to the nature of how an image is acquired with MRI, we are able to detect changes in the chemical makeup of the tissues before they are visible grossly. This allows earlier detection and treatment of injuries in our equine patients. When discussing the MRI examination, it is critical to mention the lameness exam. All horses should have the lameness localized to a specific region of the horse before being having a MRI examination. This includes specific nerve blocks or joint blocks that improve the degree of lameness after they are performed. The MRI is not a scanning tool and should not be used to image up and down the leg. For example, if a horse blocks to its heel region of the right front foot, this is a small enough area to image with MRI. Regions that can be imaged include the foot, fetlock (ankle), cannon bone, suspensory, knee, and hock. We do image both legs on almost all horses for comparison and the fact that most horses are lame bilaterally with one leg being worse than the other. Also, there is so much individual variation between some of the structures in the legs that comparison to the “normal” side helps to detect small injuries. MRI was first performed on live horses in 1997 at Washington State University. MRI allows diagnosis of both bone and soft tissue (ligament/tendon) problems that were not previously possible in horses with the use of other imaging techniques (x-rays, ultrasound, bone scan). Indications for MRI examination are those horses in which a diagnosis cannot be determined by other imaging modalities or persistent lameness that is non-responsive to treatments. Observations made with MRI on horses with lameness problems have improved our ability to make an accurate diagnosis and have increased our understanding of important clinical problems (for example, horses that are “navicular”or have a “high suspensory” injury). By far and away the biggest benefit of MRI is that it allows us to determine an accurate diagnosis. An accurate diagnosis then leads to appropriate treatment recommendations for the horse that will maximize the horse’s chance of returning to performance.

# Diagnosis of Soft Tissue Injury in the Sport Horse

### Affiliations

# Diagnosis of Soft Tissue Injury in the Sport Horse

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

For successful diagnosis of soft tissue injuries in the sport horse, localizing the area of injury during clinical and lameness evaluation will be followed in most cases by an ultrasonographic examination. With MRI more available in equine veterinary clinics, this modality can allow for a complete evaluation of soft tissue and osseous structures and is especially useful for evaluation of structures within the hoof capsule. This article discusses special ultrasonographic techniques, an overview of MRI image generation, and the use of contrast computed tomography for diagnosis of soft tissue injuries.

**Keywords:**
Horse; MRI; Soft tissue injury; Ultrasound.

--- SOURCE 5: Equine orthopedic MRI: more accurate diagnosis ---
URL: https://www.esaotevet.com/veterinary-mri/clinical-applications/equine-orthopedic-mri

SUMMARY:
This advanced imaging technique enables the rapid and precise localization of damage in both bone and soft tissues.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5ed595ccf1277768c3355277/1594673223041-0EXNJ1GI2E6JXWJX49JG/case+2.jpg?format=1000w
DESCRIPTION: A labeled MRI scan highlights the coffin joint, coffin bone, navicular bone, deep digital flexor tendon, and short pastern, with annotations indicating a T2 sag study for diagnosing equine soft tissue injuries.

IMAGE 4:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: MRI provides exceptionally detailed images, enabling visualization of both soft and hard tissues, critical for diagnosing complex equine injuries not detectable by X-rays.
MRI excels at identifying soft tissue lesions, such as tendon and ligament injuries, offering insights into conditions that traditional imaging techniques may overlook.
MRI surpasses conventional methods by allowing early detection of injuries, facilitating precise diagnoses, and minimizing invasive procedures, significantly improving treatment outcomes.
